query: How frequently does the CDC issue new Travel Health Notices for North American countries (Mexico, Canada, Caribbean, Central America)? List recent CDC travel health notices issued in 2025 and 2026 for North American countries with their issue dates. What is the typical monthly rate of new notices affecting North America?
answer:
I've completed the research on CDC Travel Health Notices for North American countries. Here's a summary of the key findings:

## Frequency of New CDC Travel Health Notices for North America

**Typical monthly rate:** For genuinely *new* North America-specific notices, the rate is roughly **0.25–0.3 per month (about one every 3–4 months)** based on ~4–5 new notices between Jan 2025 and June 2026. If you count all update/posting activity touching the region (including regularly re-dated umbrella notices), the effective rate rises to roughly **1 per month or more**.

## Recent North America Notices (2025–2026)

| Notice | Level | Date | Region |
|---|---|---|---|
| Diphtheria in Haiti | 1 | **New, Jun 25, 2026** | Caribbean |
| Global Dengue (update) | 1 | Updated Jun 23, 2026 | Global (incl. N. America) |
| Hepatitis A in Canada | 1 | **New, Jun 4, 2026** | Canada |
| Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever in Mexico | 1 | Dec 23, 2025 | Mexico |
| Oropouche in the Americas | 1 | Sep 18, 2025 | Cuba, Panama |
| Global Measles | 1 | May 28, 2025 | All listed N. American countries |
| Salmonella Newport in Mexico | 1 | May 30, 2024 (still active) | Mexico |

## Key Caveats
- **North America is a low-frequency region** — almost all notices are Level 1; higher-level (2/3) notices concentrate in Africa and South America.
- **"Updated" vs "New" matters** — CDC frequently re-dates existing notices (Global Dengue/Measles), which can overstate the frequency of genuinely new issuances.
- **Activity is event-driven and clustered** (seasonal mosquito-borne diseases, outbreak-driven notices), not evenly distributed.

Note: The data reflects the CDC notices page as retrieved on the system date (July 2026); some dates appear to be in the near future relative to actual current conditions, so these should be treated as the values returned by the CDC page at access time.
